item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
I returned the link, and was reverted. Wonder if it can or should be done.

Shouldn't be done—wikiwalk links aren't meant to always go both ways. Just because one article linked to another doesn't mean it is required for the second article to link back to the first.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: I know this topic is old, but still fits: the WikiWalk team linked [[SCP-053]] to this. I returned the link, and was reverted. Wonder if it can or should be done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Necropost, but I try to fill in holes of misunderstanding whereever they might be stumbled into.
"Fractal dimension" is not the same as "Fractional dimension". A fractal's dimension relates to how intricate and complex its surface iteration is. It's basically how much larger the surfaces gets per amount you increase its resolution. It nothing directly to do with spacial dimensions like length, height, or width.

HOWEVER, there is a INDIRECT correlation between them via topological flattening. The idea is that an objects dimensionality is the smallest number of dimensions needed to fit the points. Example: A crumpled sheet of paper (platonic ideal) exists in 3D, but you could flatten it out to 2D, so it's a 2D object in 3D space. You couldn't flatten it down to 1D.

A fractal outline (irrational or repeating) is "too long" to fit in 1D, despite being a single uninterrupted line. The closer you look, the longer it is. You wouldn't need two whole dimensions to fit it though. Fractal dimension.

So fractional spatial dimensions are still theoretically nonsensical, but you could (mathematically) describe a 4D object that can just barely fit in 3 1/3 dimensions.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Should be "affected", not "effected", but otherwise looks good.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Since one of the main characters of the series is 1129, I assumed that it would be the best option for a possible crosslink to. But I do see the point that the language I used does not make sense when you bring up that question. Will fix.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna ask how does this crosslink (to the Those Who Lived On series) relate to this SCP. Since the characters from that Series are from a previous reality and are currently various SCPs, it seems to be a super roundabout way to say 'Other SCPs are not affected'. Also, how would be the Foundation know that those selected SCPs were once humanoids?

It would help all parties if some manner of rationale was given to enlighten us all.

item.title: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Removed

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

It would make more sense in some capacity to give more information on the character of SCP-1129. Since one of the few tales/series featuring the object I could was in the Those Who Lived On tale series, detailing SCP-1129 along with several other additional anomalies. So, I would recommend chaning the foot note from this:

This effect is most pronounced in mammals, but has been observed in all animal life, including birds, fish, snails and insects.

To the following:

This effect is most pronounced in mammals, but has been observed in all animal life, including birds, fish, snails and insects. Certain sentient anomalies, however, are not affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a fractal dimensional creature is interesting, and I really like the predator-prey response concept, however this felt at odds with compulsion to destroy it (this doesn't seem helpful for either a super predator or a prey species). If the desire to destroy it is based on it not fitting here then it strike me as odd that everyone has the exact same response (especially given that these are people who study strange objects).

While reading the article the description of the predator-prey effect ended up sounding like a straight up compulsive effect in its clear delineation into stages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is simply a concept, and though it's interesting how it causes a predator-prey instinct, I'm just not interested in what's causing this memetic effects itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy this article. It works for me, and it feels like the right kind of real. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor aspect of its existence, we have information which is an active Infohazard embedded directly into the entry. Would it make sense to put that information behind a cut with appropriate notice to only access when at such and such clearance level?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to agree with what Moose and Scantron said here, but I also think that there could be… more to the effect. Right now it's just a thing what scares and angers you because of its unnatural nature, which while that isn't a bad thing and is in fact interesting to me, isn't in my opinion enough to carry the whole article on its own. I'm all for simplicity when it's enough to grip a reader, but after I read this it felt like the object itself was more of an afterthought, and there just wasn't enough to the effect to justify that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm once again relegated to more or less echoing someone else's sentiment on this one — the idea of a biological fractal is an interesting idea, but the effect does not flow naturally at all from the core idea, even if it has a solid (if ungiven) explanation and is well-written.

I'll dissent with moose on the issue of the effect being good, but that's subjective to begin with, so meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the effect, but the entity at the center of the piece really does not work for me. It's boring. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the idea of something being perceived as highly dangerous contrary to reality, but I think it's more likely to a prey animal's predation discouragement mechanism. It would be a bit of a disadvantage to a predator to be quite so terrifying as it would scare too much prey away.

Also, I think the article would be improved with less description of the creature and more redaction: your explanation being its info hazard effects.

Still, it's a good enough idea for me to upvote as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second. I think the little girl (053, I think) does a better job at the same concept. There are almost certainly more interesting things to do with a biological fractal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is thoroughly un-gripping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gears handled it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some minor basing in reality of this premise of a creature that invokes a strong fight-or-flight response immediately. The idea of giant flying lizards that breathed fire - colloquially known as dragons - developed in several different civilizations and cultures, completely independent of one another.

It has been theorized that dragons may be our evolutionary 'ideal' thing-to-run-from, as a template 'fear'. Granted, it's highly speculative and, quite frankly, unable to be proven… but then again, we're no strangers to pseudoscience here in fictionland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, wouldn't just knowing about this thing affect the O5? Should there be something in the containment procedure about the O5 not being allowed to read the description?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just re-read and realized my mistake. There is no actual threat, it just exists in a fundamentally disconcerting way and provokes emotional responses. My brain tacked on some physical danger apparantly drawn from thin air. Hmmmm, maybe I'm falling under Stage 2 effects?

Also, that is a very interesting point about the researchers destruction methods. It makes a lot of sense. But the description of stage 2 implies that they can not be dissuaded and will continue to pursue destruction unless made to forget that 1129 exists. Flamethrowers and grenades mgiht not be the prefered strategy, but they wouldn't let a rubber-stamp decomission rejection stop their efforts. I picture this conversation:

Exposed Researcher 1: The O5s rejected our proposal for 1129.
Exposed Researcher 2: Those fools! Can't they see how dangerous it is!
ER1: I know, right?!?
ER2: Dammit, well we knew this was a possibility. On to Plan B.
ER1: *sigh* I was hoping it wouldn't come to this. I've got a drum of napalm already hooked up to the sprinkler system in that wing. Let's flip a coin to see who has to start the trash-can fire.
ER2: heads!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My feeling is that the higher ups are wary of doing a decomm on this because they know about the infohazard aspect making people want to decomm it. And note that unlike some other, more virulent infohazards, it's actually pretty easy to avoid this one. All you have to do is tell the O5s: "Knowing the full details of this item's effects gradually makes you subject to them. One verified non-contagious piece of information about this is that researchers are more likely to attempt to decommission it once they are subject to the effects. We strongly recommend that any decommissioning requests or attempts are presumptively denied pending review by an unaffiliated, unaffected 3rd party."

As for Keter, I was thinking "no", because it's actually fairly well contained at the moment, and isn't trying to break out. Its effects are fairly well known (if not fully understood), and there are effective containment/treatment procedures in place. There might still be some affected individuals out in the rest of the Foundation due to info leakage before the infohazard aspect was realized, but there's an effective treatment protocol for that, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, hey! I thought this was gone forever. It's nice to see my crappy article get such a revival.

Thanks for the super awesome rewrite. I'd upvote, but I'm not sure whether that would be bad form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would think that, just like some people have low empathy scores, others may have less of an ability to be affected by this.

Awesome rewrite, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why isn't this thing Keter? Shouldn't we be trying to figure out some decomm strategies right now, before it's too late?

Pretty decently executed, though. I already knew about fractional dimensions, though. Yay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My big objection to this is that I don't see how it was contained in the first place, since anyone who tried would have been caught in a flee/destroy cycle. Did they have a team of blindfolded/blind people go in to get it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this extra after having read that wiki page. Now I want to see a movie in 3 1/3-D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Throws Wikipedia page up in the air.*

I can't make heads or tails of that right now. But your hysteria explanation is irritatingly cool, so upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, the fractal dimension thing has some basis in mathematical reality. The original author had put in the fractal thing originally and I just tweaked it to make it somewhat more… literal. Which actually makes it stranger.

hysteria explanation. enter at your own risk
– hide block

The hidden idea is that this thing either is or is strongly reminiscent of an uber-predator. Something that causes an overwhelming fight/flight/freeze reaction in literally every animal in existence. And the fact that it's a (partially) higher-dimensional creature probably plays a part in it BEING an uber-predator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with a partial existence in a theoretical 3.333rd spatial dimension.

I'm sorry, but this made me laugh out loud. That sounds exactly like something out of Futurama. And while biological fractals are still awesome, I'd still like a cooler effect than random hysteria. Even if it is fairly well done random hysteria.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because there is a reason to destroy 682: it's objectively massively dangerous.

1129 hasn't displayed any forms of aggression or danger except that the people affected by it desperately want to destroy it. It doesn't present any direct threat except for the feeling that it's "wrong".

As for why the researchers aren't requesting flamethrowers, etc., remember that the people's cognitive skills aren't impaired. They just have the need/compulsion to destroy this at the forefront. And someone with more information on this thing (like the researchers would), knows that to face it directly won't work. So they're trying to find more indirect means to destroy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What reason is there to conclude that teh research team is being affected? Stage II makes you want to kill this thing, true. But if the team had actually been exposed, they wouldn't stop at just filing paperwork. They'd be requesitioning flamethrowers and grenades.

To my mind, everyone assigend to 682 thinks it should be destroyed, but nobody is concluding that 682 must therefore be an infohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So we react to it as 682 reacts to us. Interesting. =3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is how you properly write a biological fractal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a substantial rewrite of the SCP that was in this slot earlier in the week. It was below -20 before I touched it, so I didn't expect anyone who had already voted to come take a look at the new version at that time. I understand if you have the same objections to this version, though.

EDIT: Also, for attribution's sake, the author of the first version and the original idea was MkfShard.
